meta.venue: Force signals turned into useful features

sections.title: Physical interaction can be recognized from the time-frequency content of force signals.

blocks.text: Force signals can be surprisingly expressive. With the right representation, they show when contact begins, how an action evolves, and what makes one touch pattern different from another.
A simple LEGO block assembly trial makes this concept clear: grabbing the lego, searching for the right alignment, inserting it in place and so on leave a different signature in the force signal.

blocks.alt: Semantic timeline of a LEGO block assembly and disassembly demonstration
blocks.caption: Sample LEGO block assembly demonstration showing how assembly and disassembly phases separate into recognizable force-action segments.

sections.title: Transform force signals using the wavelet transform

blocks.text: The wavelet transform decomposes force signals into time-frequency components, revealing patterns that are not visible in raw data. This makes it possible to automatically recognize different phases of physical interactions without manual tuning.

blocks.caption: The wavelet transform provides rich time-frequency features that are readily interpretable by deep learning and other ML methods.

sections.title: Why does the wavelet transform help?

items: Signals can be thought of as a linear composition of different waves at different frequencies.
Each wave may capture a specific behavior or concept in the overall signal
Each frequency has two properties, a magnitude and a phase or an alignment.

Single points of a time series cannot express the local behavior of the signal as the past history is forgotten. While a window of time may represent the signal exactly, it isn't easily interpretable by a ML algorithm and will need feature extraction.
The wavelet decomposition provides a feature descriptor for machine learning algorithms.

blocks.caption: The signals local and global behavior is described by the wavelet scalogram.

items: Recognizing actions during tactile manipulations through force sensing. Subramani, Rakita, Wang, Black, Zinn, Gleicher. IEEE/RSJ IROS, 2017.
Inferring Geometric Constraints in Human Demonstrations for Robot Programming. Guru Subramani. PhD Dissertation, 2019.
